region_of_origin: India (West Bengal / Eastern India); widely consumed across the Indian subcontinent and in the Indian diaspora in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general eggplant food product class; no specific entry for Indian shallow-fried spiced eggplant slices. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers cooked aubergine broadly; no specific code for shallow-fried spiced preparation. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for baigan bhaja as a prepared dish. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of baigan bhaja consists of 2 to 3 shallow-fried eggplant slices, weighing approximately 80 g, served as a side dish alongside rice and dal in an Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Baigan Bhaja — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F14080002 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for shallow-fried eggplant in mustard oil (~3.5 g/100 g) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baigan bhaja as a prepared dish. Eggplant (aubergine) is classified as a non-starchy vegetable with a GI of approximately 15 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Shallow-frying with oil does not materially raise GI; the high dietary fibre (5.21 g/100 g) further attenuates glycaemic response. GL per 80 g serving = (15 × 12.15 × 0.80) / 100 = 1.46, rounded to 1.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 317 mg/100 g — borderline high for a vegetable side dish
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 60–80 g and reduce added salt during preparation, as 317 mg sodium per 100 g approaches the upper threshold for a single side dish; pairing with potassium-rich foods such as dal or leafy greens can partially offset the sodium load per DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~15), high dietary fibre (5.21 g/100 g), moderate net carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baigan bhaja has a low glycaemic index (~15) and high dietary fibre (5.21 g/100 g), resulting in a glycaemic load of approximately 1.5 per 80 g serving, making it suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes when portion size is controlled to limit caloric intake from the 23.6 g/100 g fat content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (23.6 g/100 g) from shallow-frying; saturated fat estimated ~3.5 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high fat content (23.6 g/100 g) from shallow-frying raises caloric density and contributes to cardiovascular risk if consumed in large portions; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving size to 60–80 g, prefer mustard oil (rich in monounsaturated fatty acids) over saturated fats, and consider baking or grilling as a lower-fat preparation alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable (solanine alkaloids); advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) from frying
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Eggplant is a nightshade vegetable containing solanine alkaloids that may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; those with confirmed nightshade sensitivity should avoid baigan bhaja, and all RA patients should prefer baked or grilled eggplant preparations to minimise AGE formation from frying.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baigan bhaja (also spelled baingan bhaja or begun bhaja) is a traditional Indian dish consisting of eggplant (brinjal / aubergine) slices that are coated in a spice mixture — typically turmeric, red chilli powder, and salt — and shallow-fried in mustard oil or vegetable oil until golden and tender. The dish originates from West Bengal and Eastern India, where it is a staple side dish served alongside steamed rice, dal (lentil soup), and other vegetable preparations. The finished slices have a crisp, lightly charred exterior and a soft, creamy interior with a mildly smoky, spiced flavour. Nutritionally, baigan bhaja is energy-dense at 281 kcal per 100 g due to oil absorption during frying (23.6 g fat/100 g). It provides moderate protein (4.9 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (5.21 g/100 g), and meaningful iron (3.55 mg/100 g) and calcium (43 mg/100 g). Sodium is 317 mg/100 g, which is borderline high for a vegetable dish. The glycaemic index is low (~15) owing to the non-starchy nature of eggplant and its high fibre content, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. However, the high fat content warrants portion control for those managing cardiovascular disease, obesity, or caloric intake. Eggplant is a nightshade vegetable; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and nightshade sensitivity should exercise caution. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is widely consumed in the Indian diaspora communities of Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is baingan bhaja good for diabetics?
Can I eat begun bhaja with hypertension?
Is fried eggplant suitable for weight loss?
Is brinjal fry good for rheumatoid arthritis?
meal_context: Served with steamed rice and dal

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F14080002, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN reference data for shallow-fried eggplant. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (eggplant, non-starchy vegetable, GI ~15). All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for shallow-fried eggplant in mustard oil; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for this specific shallow-fried preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN nutrient tables as of 2026, and no other approved source under Project Veritas v2 source policy provides these values for baigan bhaja: monounsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not reported by HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN for this preparation; polyunsaturated_fat_g — same basis as monounsaturated_fat_g; trans_fat_g — trans fat content not reported by HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN for this preparation; potassium_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo entry for this dish; cholesterol_mg — not reported by HPB Foodo; eggplant is plant-based and cholesterol is expected to be negligible but no confirmed zero from an approved source; vitamin_c_mg — micronutrient panel not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_b6_mg — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_b12_mcg — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; plant-based dish, expected negligible but no confirmed zero from approved source; folate_mcg_dfe — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_d_mcg — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_e_mg — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; phosphorus_mg — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; magnesium_mg — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; zinc_mg — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; selenium_mcg — not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry; water_g — moisture content not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry. All -1 values confirmed unavailable in HPB Singapore Foodo (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/) and ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods tables as of 2026. 3. GI is an analogous estimate based on eggplant (non-starchy vegetable, GI ~15) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for baigan bhaja as a prepared dish has been published. 4. This dish is classified as a Singaporean-context Indian dish consumed in the Indian diaspora community; multilingual names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are provided per Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules for Singaporean dishes. 5. No core macro fields are zero; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
